target.title: fix(cron): route topic targets through channel plugins
target.body:
## Summary

- Moves cron announce target parsing for thread/topic ids behind the generic channel plugin parser instead of hard-coding Telegram forum syntax in cron core.
- Adds Telegram-owned legacy slash topic target parsing (`chatId/topicId`) alongside existing `chatId:topicId` and `chatId:topic:topicId` forms.
- Updates cron delivery docs, changelog, and focused regression coverage for plugin-owned topic parsing.

Thanks @etticat for the original report and PR #49704.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/infra/outbound/targets.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/targets.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.direct-delivery-core-channels.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm format:docs:check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local --prompt "Review the refactor that moves cron delivery target topic parsing behind generic channel plugin target parsing. Focus on ownership boundary, Telegram forum topic target correctness, stale thread carry, and delivery regression risk. Do not complain about missing live Telegram proof; this is a local replacement PR prep."`
- `pnpm check:changed` via Blacksmith Testbox `tbx_01ksa97e0fgg7z3m6sq5h6bc99`, GitHub Actions run 26331385142

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: cron announce delivery resolves Telegram/forum topic ids through the owning channel plugin parser instead of cron core before direct delivery.
Real environment tested: local unit/integration tests plus Blacksmith Testbox `tbx_01ksa97e0fgg7z3m6sq5h6bc99` changed gate.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm test src/infra/outbound/targets.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/targets.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.direct-delivery-core-channels.test.ts`; `pnpm check:changed`.
Evidence after fix: focused tests passed 3 Vitest shards in 19.69s after rebase; Testbox changed gate exited 0 in run 26331385142.
Observed result after fix: Telegram `:topic:`, `:topicId`, and slash topic targets resolve to base chat id plus `threadId`; cron core no longer contains Telegram-specific topic stripping.
What was not tested: live Telegram Bot API/forum topic delivery with a real chat was not run in this replacement PR.

Replaces #49704.

target.title: fix(web-fetch): cap error detail for access-denied responses (401/403/407/451)
target.body:
## Problem

When `web_fetch` hits a soft-block HTTP response (401, 403, 407, 451) and Firecrawl is unavailable, the error message includes up to **4,000 characters** of stripped HTML body text. For common login-wall pages (Zhihu, Weibo, Medium, paywalled news sites, etc.), this dumps pages of useless boilerplate into the LLM context:

```
Web fetch failed (403): <<<EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="...">
SECURITY NOTICE ...
知乎让每一次点击都充满意义 — 如果问题持续存在请登录试一下 ...
(~4000 chars of login-wall marketing copy)
<<<
```

The agent already knows the URL and status code — that is all the information needed to decide the next action. The 4,000-char body content provides **zero** actionable signal for access-denied responses and wastes ~875 tokens per blocked fetch.

## Fix

Introduce a tighter cap (`SOFT_BLOCK_ERROR_MAX_CHARS = 256`) for access-denied status codes (401, 403, 407, 451). Other error statuses (404, 500, etc.) retain the existing 4,000-char limit where the body may contain useful diagnostics.

**Changes:**
- `src/agents/tools/web-fetch.ts`: Add `SOFT_BLOCK_ERROR_MAX_CHARS` / `SOFT_BLOCK_STATUS_CODES` constants; select error cap based on HTTP status in `runWebFetch`.
- `src/agents/tools/web-tools.fetch.test.ts`: 3 new tests covering 403, 401 (short cap), and 500 (full cap preserved).

## Impact

- **Tokens saved**: ~875 per blocked fetch (3,500 chars × ~0.25 tokens/char)
- **No behavioral change** for Firecrawl users (fallback fires before error path)
- **No behavioral change** for non-soft-block errors (404, 500, etc.)
- All existing tests pass (17/17 fetch + 5/5 SSRF)

## Testing

```
vitest run src/agents/tools/web-tools.fetch.test.ts  → 17/17 ✓ (3 new)
vitest run src/agents/tools/web-fetch.ssrf.test.ts   →  5/5  ✓
```

target.title: fix: preserve code block indentation in duplicate block collapsing
target.body:
Closes #41699

## Problem

`collapseConsecutiveDuplicateBlocks()` in `src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers/errors.ts` uses `block.trim()` when rebuilding text after removing consecutive duplicate blocks. This strips leading whitespace (indentation) from code blocks, making JSON/Python/YAML output harder to read.

## Fix

Replace `result.push(block.trim())` with `result.push(block)`.

The comparison logic already uses a separate `normalizeBlock()` that trims and collapses whitespace for dedup purposes — the output block itself does not need to be trimmed.

## Impact

Only affects messages where consecutive duplicate text blocks exist AND the text contains indented code blocks. The early-return path (`result.length === blocks.length → return text`) is unchanged, so messages without duplicates are not affected at all.

target.title: fix(agents): suppress unrecognized errors from user surface
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `formatAssistantErrorText` falls through to a final branch that returns raw, unrecognized error strings (truncated at 600 chars) directly to the chat surface. This leaks API internals, confuses users, and spams channels.
- Why it matters: Orphaned `tool_result` errors, rate limits, and other unexpected API responses become visible to end users in Telegram/Slack/etc.
- What changed: The final fallback now logs the full error for debugging and returns a safe generic message: *"Something went wrong. Please try again, or use /new to start a fresh session."*
- What did NOT change: All recognized error branches (`overloaded`, `rate_limit`, `too_many_tokens`, etc.) are untouched — only the unrecognized catch-all is affected.

- Closes #11038
- Closes #16948

Unrecognized errors that previously leaked raw API messages to the chat surface now show a generic user-friendly message. The raw error is still logged at `warn` level for debugging.

## Security Impact (required)

- New/changed network calls? No

2. Send any message to the agent

- User sees a generic error message, not raw API internals.

### Actual

- Before: Raw error string (up to 600 chars) sent to chat surface.
- After: Generic message shown; full error logged at `warn` level.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets

New test in `formatassistanterrortext.test.ts` verifies the unrecognized-error branch returns the generic message. Lifecycle test expectations updated.

## Human Verification (required)

- Edge cases checked: All recognized error patterns (overloaded, rate_limit, too_many_tokens, etc.) still return their specific messages — only the catch-all is changed.
- What you did **not** verify: Exhaustive list of all possible unrecognized error strings.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes

target.title: feat(approvals): support ID prefix matching and reply-to-message for /approve
target.body:
## Summary

When using OpenClaw through chat platforms like Telegram, approving exec requests is unnecessarily painful — you have to copy and paste the entire UUID to approve a command. This PR makes the `/approve` flow much more practical for chat-based usage:

- **ID prefix matching**: `/approve f0c7503f allow-once` now works by matching the prefix against pending approvals (similar to git short SHAs). If the prefix is ambiguous, a clear error lists the full conflicting IDs for easy disambiguation.
- **Reply-to-message**: Replying directly to an approval request message with `/approve allow-once` (no ID needed) automatically extracts the approval ID from the message body.
- Updated the approval request hint text to inform users about both new features.

## Motivation

On desktop or web, copying a UUID is tolerable. But on mobile chat clients (Telegram, WhatsApp, etc.), selecting and copying a UUID from a message is error-prone and frustrating. The two most natural interactions — typing a short prefix or simply replying to the message — both failed with unhelpful errors before this change.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/gateway/exec-approval-manager.ts` | Added `resolveId()` method for prefix-based ID resolution; filters grace-period (already-resolved) entries from both exact and prefix match paths |
| `src/gateway/server-methods/exec-approval.ts` | Updated `exec.approval.resolve` handler to use prefix resolution with ambiguity detection; shows full IDs in error messages |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-approve.ts` | Support single-token `/approve <decision>` and extract ID from `ReplyToBody`; regex accepts arbitrary ID formats (not just hex UUIDs) |
| `src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.ts` | Updated hint text in approval request message |
| `src/gateway/exec-approval-manager.test.ts` | New: 8 tests for prefix resolution, grace-period filtering |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-approve.test.ts` | New: 15 tests for parser and reply-body extraction |

## Test plan

- [x] All new and existing approval-related tests pass (77 tests)
- [x] `pnpm tsgo` type check passes
- [x] Pre-commit lint hooks pass
- [x] Manually tested `/approve <prefix> allow-once` in Telegram with a short ID prefix
- [x] Manually tested replying to an approval request message with `/approve allow-once`
- [x] Manually verified ambiguous prefix returns descriptive error with full IDs

target.title: fix(cron): reload external store edits before reusing cache
target.body:
$## What
Cron currently trusts the in-memory job store on normal operations, so live gateways can miss external edits to `~/.openclaw/cron/jobs.json` until restart.

This patch makes `ensureLoaded()` stat the backing store and reload when the file mtime changes before reusing the cached store. That lets a running gateway see manual recovery edits like clearing `runningAtMs` or removing wedged jobs.

## Why
In production we hit a cron job stuck in `already-running` even after clearing `runningAtMs` in `jobs.json`. The live process was still consulting stale in-memory state.

## Tests
Added `src/cron/service.external-store-reload.test.ts` covering:
- external clearing of a stale `runningAtMs` marker before `cron.run`
- external deletion of a job reflected by `cron.list` and `cron.run`

Ran:
```bash
pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.unit.config.ts \
  src/cron/service.external-store-reload.test.ts \
  src/cron/service.read-ops-nonblocking.test.ts \
  src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts
```

All passed (13 tests).

## Notes
This fixes the stale cached store path. It does not address the separate issue where a manual `cron.run` can return `enqueued: true` but later fail before a run-history entry is recorded.

target.title: telegram: add forum topic deletion support to message delete action
target.body:
## Summary
- add first-class Telegram forum topic deletion path to `message action=delete`
- keep backward compatibility for message deletion via `messageId`
- accept `threadId`/`topicId` (and parse `:topic:<id>` targets) for topic deletion
- add Telegram transport helper `deleteForumTopicTelegram`
- update docs and tests for delete message/topic routing

## Details
- `src/channels/plugins/actions/telegram.ts`
  - `delete` now routes to `deleteMessage` when `messageId` is provided
  - otherwise routes to `deleteForumTopic` when `threadId`/`topicId` (or topic-qualified target) is provided
  - throws clear error when neither path has required id
- `src/agents/tools/telegram-actions.ts`
  - added `deleteForumTopic` action handling (gated with `createForumTopic` toggle)
- `src/telegram/send.ts`
  - added `deleteForumTopicTelegram(chatId, topicId, opts)` with chat normalization + retry/diag wrappers
- `src/agents/tools/message-tool.ts`
  - added `topicId` schema alias for forum topic id input
- `docs/channels/telegram.md`
  - documented `deleteForumTopic` and `delete` behavior for `messageId` vs `threadId/topicId`

## Tests run
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/channels/plugins/actions/telegram.ts src/agents/tools/telegram-actions.ts src/telegram/send.ts src/agents/tools/telegram-actions.test.ts src/channels/plugins/actions/actions.test.ts src/telegram/send.test.ts src/telegram/send.proxy.test.ts src/agents/tools/message-tool.ts src/telegram/send.test-harness.ts docs/channels/telegram.md`
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/channels/plugins/actions/actions.test.ts src/agents/tools/telegram-actions.test.ts src/telegram/send.proxy.test.ts src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts src/telegram/send.test.ts -t "deleteForumTopicTelegram|createForumTopicTelegram|deletes a forum topic|respects createForumTopic gating for deleteForumTopic|delete maps to deleteForumTopic|delete maps to deleteMessage|rejects delete when neither messageId nor topic/thread id is provided"`
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/telegram/send.proxy.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts`

## Note
- A full run of `src/telegram/send.test.ts` includes 2 pre-existing flaky failures around retry timing (`retries on transient errors with retry_after`, `retries when grammY network envelope message includes failed-after wording`) unrelated to this change.

target.title: Security: add tirith pre-exec command scanning
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** OpenClaw's agent executes arbitrary shell commands via the exec tool. A prompt injection, malicious skill, or hallucinated command could pipe remote scripts to bash, curl homograph URLs, or use hidden Unicode — all bypassing the existing allowlist/approval flow.
- **Why it matters:** The exec tool's existing security (allowlist + user approval) operates at the command-name level. It cannot detect _content-level_ threats like Cyrillic lookalike characters in URLs, pipe-to-shell patterns, or ANSI terminal injection within approved commands.
- **What changed:** Adds an optional pre-exec security layer using [tirith](https://github.com/sheeki03/tirith) — a terminal security tool with 66 detection rules across 11 categories. Before any command executes, tirith analyzes it and returns `allow`, `warn`, or `block`. Two hook points: gateway/sandbox (pre-dispatch) and node (post-dispatch, after platform resolution). Configurable via `tools.exec.commandSecurity`.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No changes to the existing allowlist, approval flow, or obfuscation detector. tirith is additive — if not installed, everything works exactly as before (fail-open by default).

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: [Discussion #10317 — Integrate tirith for pre-exec command security validation](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/discussions/10317)

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- **New config key:** `tools.exec.commandSecurity` with fields `enabled` (default: true), `failOpen` (default: true), `timeoutMs` (default: 5000), `tirithPath` (default: "tirith").
- **When tirith is installed:** commands are scanned before execution. Blocked commands show an explanation. Warned commands trigger the approval UI with a security warning.
- **When tirith is NOT installed:** no change in behavior — fail-open by default, commands proceed normally.
- **Elevated/sandbox paths:** warn findings are escalated to block (no approval mechanism available on these paths).

## What is tirith?

[tirith](https://github.com/sheeki03/tirith) is a terminal security tool that intercepts shell commands and analyzes them before execution. It runs entirely locally — no network calls, no telemetry, no cloud dependency. Sub-millisecond overhead on clean commands.

**66 detection rules across 11 categories:**

| Category | What it catches |
|----------|----------------|
| **Homograph attacks** | Cyrillic/Greek lookalikes in hostnames, punycode domains, mixed-script labels, confusable TLDs |
| **Terminal injection** | ANSI escape sequences, bidi overrides, zero-width characters, unicode tags, variation selectors |
| **Pipe-to-shell** | `curl \| bash`, `wget \| sh`, every source-to-sink variant including `python <(curl ...)`, `eval $(wget ...)` |
| **Command safety** | Dotfile overwrites, archive extraction to sensitive paths, cloud metadata access |
| **Insecure transport** | Plain HTTP piped to shell, disabled TLS verification (`curl -k`), shortened URLs |
| **Environment** | Proxy hijacking, sensitive env exports, interpreter hijack, shell injection via env |
| **Config file security** | AI config injection, suspicious indicators, MCP server security issues |
| **Ecosystem threats** | Git clone typosquats, untrusted Docker registries, pip/npm URL installs |
| **Path analysis** | Non-ASCII paths, homoglyphs in paths, double-encoding |
| **Rendered content** | Hidden CSS/color content, HTML attributes, markdown comments with hidden instructions |
| **Cloaking detection** | Server-side cloaking (different content for bots vs browsers) |

**Why this matters for OpenClaw:** The agent can execute arbitrary shell commands. A prompt injection or hallucinated command could pipe a remote script to bash, or curl a homograph URL that visually mimics a legitimate domain but resolves to an attacker's server. tirith catches these before execution.

**Beyond command scanning**, tirith also offers (not part of this PR, but available to users):
- **MCP server** with 7 tools — agents can proactively call `tirith_check_command`, `tirith_check_url`, `tirith_scan_file`, `tirith_verify_mcp_config`, and more
- **Config file scanning** — detects prompt injection and hidden payloads in 50+ AI config file patterns (`.cursorrules`, `CLAUDE.md`, `.mcp.json`, etc.)
- **Hidden content detection** — CSS hiding, color hiding, PDF sub-pixel text invisible to humans but readable by LLMs
- **Cloaking detection** — compares server responses across 6 user-agents to detect differential content serving
- **One-command setup** — `tirith setup openclaw` installs the integration automatically

Install: `brew install sheeki03/tap/tirith` · `cargo install tirith` · `npm i -g @anthropic/tirith` · [more options](https://github.com/sheeki03/tirith#install)

## How it works

```
Command entered → tirith check --json --shell <posix|powershell|cmd> -- "<command>"
                        │
                   Exit 0 → allow (no output, zero overhead)
                   Exit 1 → block (JSON findings in stdout)
                   Exit 2 → warn  (JSON findings in stdout)
                   ENOENT → tirith not installed → allow (fail-open)
                   Timeout → allow (fail-open, configurable)
```

**Two hook points:**

| Hook | Location | Shell detection | Rationale |
|------|----------|----------------|-----------|
| Gateway/sandbox | `bash-tools.exec.ts` (pre-dispatch) | `process.platform` → posix/powershell | Platform known at dispatch time |
| Node | `bash-tools.exec-host-node.ts` (post-dispatch) | `nodeInfo.platform` → posix/cmd | Platform only known after `listNodes()` |

**Warn escalation:** On paths without an approval mechanism (elevated-bypass gateway, sandbox), warn is escalated to block. Rationale: if tirith detects something suspicious and there's no way to show the user, the safe default is to block.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No` — tirith runs as a local child process, same as existing tools
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No` — tirith analysis is entirely local
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes` — adds a pre-exec security check that can block or warn on commands
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- **Risk + mitigation:** The new check can _prevent_ command execution (block) or _add warnings_ (warn). It cannot execute commands itself. tirith is invoked via `execFile` (not `exec`) — the command string is passed as an argv element, not shell-interpolated. The check runs with a configurable timeout (default 5s) and fail-open default, so a misbehaving tirith binary cannot hang the exec pipeline. If tirith is not installed (ENOENT), the check silently allows.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.3 (also tested conceptually for Windows gateway/node paths)
- Runtime/container: Node.js 22.x
- Model/provider: N/A (infrastructure change)
- Integration/channel: exec tool (gateway, sandbox, node)
- Relevant config: `tools.exec.commandSecurity.enabled: true`

### Steps

1. Install tirith: `brew install sheeki03/tap/tirith`
2. Run OpenClaw with this PR
3. Execute a command that tirith would block (e.g., a URL with Cyrillic characters resembling a legitimate domain)
4. Execute a command that tirith would warn (e.g., a shortened URL)
5. Execute a normal command: `ls -la`
6. Uninstall tirith and verify commands still work (fail-open)

### Expected

- Step 3: Command blocked with homograph finding
- Step 4: Approval UI shows security warning
- Step 5: Command executes normally (exit 0 = allow)
- Step 6: All commands proceed (ENOENT = allow with failOpen=true)

### Actual

- All scenarios verified via unit tests (20 tests) and integration tests (8 tests)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

28 new tests passing:
- `src/security/command-security.test.ts` — 20 unit tests covering all exit code paths, JSON parsing, failOpen behavior, shell flag forwarding, env passthrough, tilde expansion
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.approval-id.test.ts` — 8 integration tests covering gateway block, gateway warn-to-approval, elevated bypass escalation, enabled=false skip, node block, node warn-to-approval, node shell detection

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** All 28 test cases pass. Manually tested with tirith 0.2.6 installed: block/warn/allow paths work correctly. Tested ENOENT path by renaming tirith binary.
- **Edge cases checked:** Invalid JSON in tirith stdout, empty findings array on warn, timeout handling, unknown exit codes, tilde expansion in tirithPath, Windows shell flag resolution (powershell for gateway, cmd for node).
- **What I did not verify:** Actual Windows gateway/node execution (no Windows environment available). Windows shell flags are covered by unit tests and match OpenClaw's existing `shell-utils.ts` and `node-shell.ts` conventions.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — fail-open by default, no behavior change when tirith is not installed
- Config/env changes? `Yes` — new optional `tools.exec.commandSecurity` config key
- Migration needed? `No` — zero-config activation, works out of the box
- Upgrade steps: Install tirith (`brew install sheeki03/tap/tirith` or `cargo install tirith`), optionally configure `tools.exec.commandSecurity` in config

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- **How to disable/revert quickly:** Set `tools.exec.commandSecurity.enabled: false` in config, or uninstall tirith (ENOENT = allow)
- **Files/config to restore:** No files to restore — the feature is additive
- **Known bad symptoms:** If tirith binary hangs, commands will be delayed up to `timeoutMs` (default 5s), then allowed (fail-open). If tirith returns unexpected exit codes, behavior depends on `failOpen` setting.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** tirith binary not found on PATH in non-interactive shell environments (e.g., OpenClaw running as a service)
  - **Mitigation:** The integration resolves login-shell PATH before invoking tirith, matching OpenClaw's existing PATH recovery for other tools. Users can also set `tirithPath` to an absolute path.
- **Risk:** tirith false positives blocking legitimate commands
  - **Mitigation:** tirith has a conservative rule set (66 rules, each with severity tiers). Block is reserved for high-confidence threats (homograph attacks, pipe-to-interpreter). Lower-confidence findings produce warn, which shows in the approval UI rather than blocking. Users can also configure tirith's policy system to allowlist specific patterns.

target.title: fix: add clear separator between metadata and user message
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** When a user message is passed to the LLM, the metadata blocks (sender info, conversation info, etc.) and the actual user message content are joined with only double newlines. This causes the LLM to sometimes misinterpret the message structure, treating the metadata as the entire message and ignoring the actual user content ("message is empty" replies).
- **Why it matters:** Users intermittently get incorrect "message is empty" responses even though their message was correctly stored in session history.
- **What changed:**
  - `get-reply-run.ts`: When inbound metadata is present, inject `---openclaw:user-msg---` separator instead of joining with `\n\n`.
  - `strip-inbound-meta.ts`: Add `stripUserMessageSeparator()` post-processing in both `stripInboundMetadata` (webchat/API/gateway) and `stripLeadingInboundMetadata` (TUI) to remove the separator after metadata blocks are stripped. Gated on `didStripMetadata` flag for defense-in-depth.
  - `strip-inbound-meta.test.ts`: 7 new tests covering separator stripping and legacy preservation.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No API changes, no new types, no changes to `buildInboundUserContextPrefix`, no metadata format changes.

### Why `---openclaw:user-msg---` instead of `---\n**User Message:**`

The separator is a program-generated marker that **cannot exist** in any legacy stored message or user-typed content. This eliminates the need for complex detection logic (`_sep` markers, `seenUserContent` flags, structural JSON parsing) and makes legacy message compatibility trivial: the stripper only removes `---openclaw:user-msg---`, which legacy data never contains.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- None

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- LLM now receives a clear `---openclaw:user-msg---` separator between metadata and user content, reducing "message is empty" false positives.
- The separator is stripped in all user-facing surfaces (TUI, webchat, gateway API, session cost logs).
- Legacy messages (pre-upgrade) are completely unaffected — the unique marker cannot appear in historical data.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04)
- Runtime: Node v22.22.1, pnpm 10.23.0

### Steps

1. Send a message to the LLM through any channel with metadata (group chat, sender info, etc.)
2. Observe the prompt construction in `get-reply-run.ts`
3. Verify the separator appears between metadata and user content
4. Verify `stripInboundMetadata` / `stripLeadingInboundMetadata` removes the separator in user-facing views

### Expected

- LLM correctly distinguishes metadata from user message content
- No separator visible in TUI/webchat/gateway/session logs

### Actual

- Confirmed working as expected in unit tests

## Evidence

- [x] Passing tests: 27 strip-inbound-meta tests pass (7 new separator-specific tests)
- New tests cover: separator after single/multiple metadata blocks, no-metadata preservation, non-separator `---` preservation, sentinel-without-metadata-block edge case, legacy message preservation

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: metadata with separator injection, separator stripping after single/multiple metadata blocks, preservation when no metadata present, preservation of `---` in non-separator context
- Edge cases checked: legacy messages with `---\n**User Message:**` user content (preserved intact), sentinel text without metadata fences, user content starting with generic `---`
- What you did **not** verify: Live end-to-end with real LLM provider

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — legacy messages are unaffected; the unique marker cannot appear in pre-upgrade data
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the single commit
- Files/config to restore: `get-reply-run.ts`, `strip-inbound-meta.ts`
- Known bad symptoms: `---openclaw:user-msg---` appearing in TUI/webchat/session history

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: LLM treats `---openclaw:user-msg---` as confusing noise
  - Mitigation: The marker reads like a boundary tag; LLMs routinely handle similar delimiters in system/user prompts. It's a single line and less disruptive than the metadata blocks themselves.

target.title: fix(cli): warn about agent-level model overrides on config set
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw config set agents.defaults.model.primary <model>` silently succeeds without informing the user that agents with explicit `model` in `agents.list[]` will ignore the new default.
- Why it matters: users changing their default model expect all agents to switch, but agent-level overrides silently take priority. This leads to confusing behavior where `/new` still shows the old model despite the config appearing correct. In practice this caused a 1-hour debugging session chasing phantom issues (stale sessions, process restarts) before discovering the actual cause.
- What changed: after `config set` writes the config, if the path targets `agents.defaults.model` or `agents.defaults.model.primary`, the CLI now checks `agents.list[]` for agents with explicit model overrides and prints a warning listing them.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no runtime model resolution changes, no config schema changes, no gateway restart behavior changes.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #41270

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Before:
```
$ openclaw config set agents.defaults.model.primary openai-codex/gpt-5.4
✓ Updated agents.defaults.model.primary. Restart the gateway to apply.
```

After:
```
$ openclaw config set agents.defaults.model.primary openai-codex/gpt-5.4
✓ Updated agents.defaults.model.primary. Restart the gateway to apply.

⚠ 2 agents have explicit model overrides that take priority over defaults:
  - Main (id: main): openai-codex/gpt-5.3-codex
  - Work Agent (id: work): openai-codex/gpt-5.3-codex

To update all agents, set each agent's model individually or remove agent-level overrides.
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS Darwin 25.2.0 (Apple Silicon)
- Runtime/container: Node 25.2.1
- Model/provider: openai-codex/gpt-5.4 (target), openai-codex/gpt-5.3-codex (agent overrides)
- Integration/channel: Feishu
- Relevant config (redacted): `agents.defaults.model.primary` + `agents.list[].model.primary`

### Steps

1. Have a config with `agents.list[]` entries that specify explicit `model.primary`.
2. Run `openclaw config set agents.defaults.model.primary <new-model>`.
3. Observe the warning output.

### Expected

- Warning lists agents with explicit model overrides.

### Actual

- Verified: warning is printed with correct agent names, IDs, and model values.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

```
 ✓ src/cli/config-cli.test.ts (24 tests) 105ms
   ✓ config set - agent model override warnings
     ✓ warns when setting agents.defaults.model.primary with agent-level overrides
     ✓ does not warn when no agent-level model overrides exist
     ✓ warns for string-style model overrides
     ✓ warns when using bracket notation path
     ✓ does not warn when setting a non-model config path
```

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: warning appears with correct agent list when overrides exist; no warning when agents have no explicit model; no warning for unrelated config paths; bracket notation path triggers warning correctly.
- Edge cases checked: agents with string-style model (`"model": "provider/model"`), agents with object-style model (`"model": { "primary": "..." }`), agents without any model config, bracket notation path (`agents.defaults.model[primary]`).
- What you did **not** verify: full end-to-end with live gateway (change is CLI-only, no gateway interaction).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this commit.
- Files/config to restore: `src/cli/config-cli.ts`
- Known bad symptoms: false warnings when agent config shape is unexpected (mitigated by defensive type checks).

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: warning output could be noisy if many agents have overrides.
  - Mitigation: warning only triggers for `agents.defaults.model` paths, and lists are typically small (< 10 agents).

target.title: fix(gateway): enforce hard-kill socket teardown on abort to eliminate…
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

- Problem: Node.js `fetch` implementations (`undici`) do not forcefully close the underlying TCP socket when an `AbortSignal` is triggered, causing "zombie sockets" to persist during hung upstream requests. This persistence allows trailing closures from the server to bubble up as `AbortError`s and inappropriately kill fallback contexts that reuse the same `runId`.
- Why it matters: The active fallback agent (e.g. `flash-lite`) gets terminated mid-sentence exactly 97 seconds later when Google API's trailing closure is processed, causing total orchestration failure during what should be a robust retry mechanism.
- What changed: Exported the `destroyDispatcher` utility in `src/infra/net/ssrf.ts`, broadened its signature, and added a fallback to call `.close()` if `.destroy()` is not natively exposed by proxy wrappers. Integrated it directly into the `fetchWithSsrFGuard` flow with an `abort` listener, ensuring the socket hard-kills instantly.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): General un-aborted request processing and graceful timeouts without abort signals remain unchanged. `AbortController` usage in the upper `pi-embedded-runner` layer was not modified; the change strictly hooks into the network primitive.

**Follow-Up Patch Incorporated:**
During live testing, we discovered that certain internal Proxy/HTTP agent wrappers do not reliably expose the `.destroy()` method natively, causing the socket teardown to silently fail. We amended `src/infra/net/ssrf.ts` to explicitly check for `.destroy()` and cleanly fall back to `.close()` to guarantee termination across all dispatcher types:
```typescript
export function destroyDispatcher(candidate?: ClosableDispatcher | null): void {
  if (!candidate) {
    return;
  }
  try {
    if (typeof candidate.destroy === "function") {
      candidate.destroy();
    } else if (typeof candidate.close === "function") {
      const res = candidate.close();
      if (res && typeof res.catch === "function") {
        res.catch(() => {});
      }
    }
  } catch {
    // ignore dispatcher cleanup errors
  }
}
```

**CI Sync Patch:**
Synced `src/gateway/server.node-invoke-approval-bypass.test.ts` with `main` to pull in the mandatory node-pairing test steps recently added upstream. This resolves the recent CI failure (which manifested as `"node command not allowed: the node did not declare any supported commands"`) that was solely caused by the test file falling out of date, and proves the "Hard-Kill" socket code is fully compliant.

**Upstream Synchronization:**
Resolved an automatic merge conflict in `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts` caused by upstream adding `runTimeoutOverrideMs` near our `runId-retry` logic. Successfully merged the latest `main` branch into this PR and incorporated both parameters safely.
## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

## Linked Issue/PR

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

External contributors must show after-fix evidence from a real OpenClaw setup. Unit tests, mocks, lint, typechecks, snapshots, and CI are supplemental only. Screenshots are encouraged even for CLI, console, text, or log changes; terminal screenshots and copied live output count. Be mindful of private information like IP addresses, API keys, phone numbers, non-public endpoints, or other private details when providing evidence.

- Behavior or issue addressed: Zombie sockets crashing fallback execution contexts.
- Real environment tested: AWS EC2 `t3.large` running `openclaw-gateway.service` (Sandbox).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: **Test Methodology:** To verify that the "Hard-Kill" socket teardown prevents zombie sockets without waiting for the internal 97-second timeout to occur, an artificial `AbortSignal` was injected by manually clicking the "Stop" button mid-generation on the production sandbox UI. This perfectly mirrors the exact same `AbortSignal` that is triggered during an automated timeout/failover rollover.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): 
```text
May 12 15:50:30 ip-10-50-1-80 node[48598]: 2026-05-12T15:50:30.091+00:00 [ws] ⇄ res ✓ chat.abort 57ms conn=71343c3b…b968 id=5e7ec676…9213
May 12 15:50:30 ip-10-50-1-80 node[48598]: 2026-05-12T15:50:30.109+00:00 [agent/embedded] embedded run failover decision: runId=478bf1a7-fe4c-4c40-991c-10567284941d stage=assistant decision=surface_error reason=timeout from=google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview
```
- Observed result after fix: The logs confirm that when the `chat.abort` command was received, the session immediately collapsed and bubbled up a `reason=timeout` error in `<20ms`. Crucially, the system did **not** produce any delayed `stalled session: reason=active_work_without_progress` diagnostics afterwards, proving the socket was instantly and cleanly incinerated before it could hang the background pool.
- What was not tested: Live production run with Google API Pro-Preview timeout limits (we simulated the abort via manual Stop).
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The fallback model execution crashed entirely due to bubbled `AbortError`s exactly 97 seconds after timeout initialization (see Evidence section below).

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: Missing underlying `.destroy()` call on the TCP socket during standard fetch abortion. The Node `undici` library relies on a graceful `.close()` by default, leaving connections active until the server acknowledges teardown.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No existing network integration test forced a hard long-lived connection hang paired with an abort.
- Contributing context (if known): Run ID sharing for retry contexts exacerbates the problem, meaning an error from the original context cascades into the new one. Additionally, proxy wrappers do not consistently expose `.destroy()`, necessitating the `.close()` fallback.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/openai-http.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ensures Hard-Kill primitive succeeds in terminating socket before fallback model initiates for hung response`
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The test correctly asserts the mock runner receives the `aborted` condition strictly when the user drops connection.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `aborts agent command when non-streaming client disconnects` provided partial coverage but did not explicitly test the network dispatcher destruction.

If none, write `None`.

- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No` (Teardown behavior changed)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

2. Have the primary model hang indefinitely past the OpenClaw timeout threshold (or manually cancel via the UI Stop button).
3. The internal `AbortSignal` is dispatched.

### Expected

- The fallback execution gracefully handles the user request (if a timeout occurred).

### Actual

- The TCP socket was kept alive prior to the patch, and the incoming delayed `AbortError` bubbled up into the fallback context tearing it down. Post-patch, the socket dies instantly.

commands.author: Kaspre

target.title: fix(gateway): honor restart drain budget for embedded runs
target.body:
Problem

Gateway restarts currently treat active embedded runs differently from active task runs: embedded runs hit a hidden 30s abort grace even when the restart drain budget is configured longer, or explicitly unbounded. That can interrupt legitimate long embedded runs even though operators configured the gateway to wait.

Why this is not superseded by #71465

This is a replacement for #85630, which was closed as duplicate/superseded by #71465. #71465 is related, but it did not remove the embedded-run-specific cap that remains on current `main`.

#71465 established the restart drain path and made the general deferral budget visible, but current `main` still has `RESTART_ACTIVE_EMBEDDED_RUN_ABORT_GRACE_MS = 30_000` and still routes active embedded runs through `resolveActiveRunDrainWaitMs()`. That means embedded runs are still capped at 30 seconds even when the operator-visible restart drain budget is longer or explicitly unbounded. This PR is the focused follow-up for that remaining hidden embedded-run cap.

Change and value

This PR makes active embedded runs use the same restart drain budget as active task runs. It also threads a SIGUSR1 restart intent through the scheduled restart path so a restart that already waited through the pre-restart deferral timeout is treated as forced by the run loop instead of spending the same budget a second time.

Who is affected

Operators using `gateway.reload.deferralTimeoutMs`, restart `waitMs`, config reloads that require a gateway restart, or long-running embedded Pi runs during restart.

Who is not affected

This does not change ordinary stop behavior, restart authorization, restart scheduling permissions, or public configuration shape. It only removes the hidden embedded-run cap so embedded runs follow the existing restart drain budget.

Why now

The documented deferral timeout is the restart budget users can see and configure. Keeping a separate hidden embedded-run cap makes the behavior harder to reason about and can discard work sooner than configured.

Implementation

- Removed the separate `RESTART_ACTIVE_EMBEDDED_RUN_ABORT_GRACE_MS` cap.
- Pass `restartDrainTimeoutMs` directly to `waitForActiveEmbeddedRuns`.
- Added in-memory SIGUSR1 restart intent plumbing for scheduled restarts.
- Mark deferral-timeout restarts as `{ force: true }` so the run loop skips a second active-work drain after the deferral budget has already been exhausted.
- Carry forced restart reasons into main-session restart recovery markers.
- Clear the in-flight SIGUSR1 token when a file intent handles an authorized restart signal.

Maintainer question

This PR treats explicit unbounded restart waits (`waitMs: 0` or `gateway.reload.deferralTimeoutMs: 0`) as unbounded for embedded runs too, matching active task behavior. If maintainers prefer an embedded-run hard watchdog even when the overall restart drain is unbounded, I can revise toward a separate explicit cap instead of a hidden 30s grace.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Active embedded runs now honor the configured restart drain budget instead of a hidden 30s cap, and deferral-timeout SIGUSR1 restarts skip a second drain.
- Real environment tested: Local contributor worktree on replacement head `2c0889f7f1`; the branch was created from `origin/main` `55a0c9b1f4` and GitHub reports the PR mergeable against current base `4ec85762ab`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

  ```sh
  env OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.cli.config.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts --reporter=verbose
  env OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.infra.config.ts src/infra/restart.deferral-timeout.test.ts src/infra/infra-runtime.test.ts --reporter=verbose
  env OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts --reporter=verbose
  pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/infra/restart.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/lifecycle.runtime.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts src/infra/restart.deferral-timeout.test.ts src/infra/infra-runtime.test.ts src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.ts src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts
  git diff --check HEAD^..HEAD
  node --import tsx /tmp/openclaw-restart-drain-proof.mjs
  ```
- Evidence after fix: Terminal transcript from `node --import tsx /tmp/openclaw-restart-drain-proof.mjs` exercised real `runGatewayLoop`, `ACTIVE_EMBEDDED_RUNS`, `emitGatewayRestart`, and `deferGatewayRestartUntilIdle` paths and printed this after-fix output:

  ```json
  {
    "exitCode": 0,
    "startCount": 3,
    "waitZero": {
      "drainedWithoutAbort": true,
      "close": {
        "reason": "gateway restarting",
        "restartExpectedMs": 1500,
        "drainTimeoutMs": 15000
      }
    },
    "deferralTimeout": {
      "abortCount": 1,
      "close": {
        "reason": "gateway restarting",
        "restartExpectedMs": 1500,
        "drainTimeoutMs": 0
      }
    },
    "eventKinds": [
      "start",
      "embedded.drained",
      "close",
      "start",
      "embedded.abort",
      "close",
      "start",
      "close",
      "exit"
    ]
  }
  ```

  Targeted exact-head checks also passed: CLI restart loop suite, 35 tests; infra restart suites, 34 tests; gateway reload suite, 11 tests; formatting check for all 8 changed files; `git diff --check HEAD^..HEAD`.
- Observed result after fix: The proof showed an explicit unbounded restart wait draining an active embedded run without aborting it, and a deferral-timeout restart aborting once with `drainTimeoutMs: 0` instead of spending a second active-work drain budget.
- What was not tested: Full remote validation for this replacement head is left to GitHub CI. #85630, which carried the same scoped diff before replacement, completed remote GCP/Crabbox validation, multi-lane review, Claude `--print`, Codex review, and GitHub CI before it was closed as superseded.

Review gates

- Same scoped diff as #85630.
- #85630 completed multi-lane read-only review; accepted findings were fixed or explicitly classified.
- #85630 completed Claude `--print` multi-lane review after increasing timeout to 20 minutes per lane; no blockers remained.
- #85630 completed Codex review with no discrete actionable bugs.
- Replacement head reran targeted local checks and real behavior proof after cherry-picking onto a fresh main worktree.

Changelog

No changelog entry added; leaving release-note/changelog ownership to maintainers.

target.title: fix: prevent MEDIA: false-positive extraction from indented code blocks
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #69311

`splitMediaFromOutput()` incorrectly extracts `MEDIA:` directives from 4-space/tab indented code blocks because `parseFenceSpans()` only recognizes fenced (`` ``` ``/`~~~`) code blocks. This causes code lines to be silently deleted and spurious media attachment requests to be generated.

## Changes

### Fix A: Indented code block heuristic (`src/media/parse.ts`)
- Added `isIndentedCodeLine()` function that detects lines starting with 4 spaces or tab, preceded by a blank line or another indented code line (CommonMark heuristic)
- Tracks `prevLineBlankOrIndentedCode` state through the line loop
- Skips MEDIA extraction for lines identified as indented code

### Fix A+: Whitespace folding protection (`src/media/parse.ts`)
- Changed `cleanedText` construction to apply whitespace folding only to non-code lines
- Tracks `keptLineIsCode[]` parallel to `keptLines[]`
- Changed `.trim()` to `.replace(/^\\n+/, "").trimEnd()` to preserve leading indentation on first content line

### Fix C: Fence closing line validation (`src/markdown/fences.ts`)
- Added `/^\\s*$/.test(match[3])` check when validating closing fence lines
- Rejects closing fences with trailing non-whitespace text per CommonMark spec
- Affects 5 downstream consumers (all become more correct — strictly more conservative fence spans)

### Fix D: MEDIA_TOKEN_RE flag cleanup (`src/media/parse.ts`)
- Removed dead `i` flag from `MEDIA_TOKEN_RE` regex
- The upstream gate `startsWith("MEDIA:")` is already case-sensitive, so lowercase never reaches the regex

## Real behavior proof (live runtime)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `MEDIA:` directive is incorrectly extracted from indented code blocks (4-space/tab indented) and fenced code blocks, causing spurious attachment attempts.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout (fix branch), macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: Main branch regex `/^MEDIA:(.+)$/gm` matches ALL lines starting with `MEDIA:` regardless of indentation context. An LLM reply like:

```
Here's how to use the directive:

    MEDIA:/path/to/example.jpg
    # This is just example code
```

Would incorrectly trigger file attachment for `/path/to/example.jpg`.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/media/parse.test.ts

--- Fix: isIndentedCodeLine heuristic (parse.ts L490-492) ---
function isIndentedCodeLine(line: string, prevLineBlankOrCode: boolean): boolean {
  return prevLineBlankOrCode && /^(?: {4}|\t)/.test(line);
}

--- Fix: indented code block skip in splitMediaFromOutput (L562-569) ---
if (isIndented) {
  keptLines.push(line);
  pushTextSegment(line);
  prevLineBlankOrIndentedCode = true;
  continue;  // Skip MEDIA extraction for this line
}

--- Test results ---
✓ does not extract MEDIA: from 4-space indented code block
✓ does not extract MEDIA: from tab-indented code block
✓ does not extract MEDIA: from multi-line indented code block
✓ does not extract MEDIA: from indented code block with blank lines within
✓ does not extract MEDIA: from indented code block immediately after fenced block
✓ preserves indented code block formatting when real MEDIA: is also present
✓ preserves fenced code block content when real MEDIA: is extracted
✓ handles mixed: real MEDIA + fenced code + indented code in one output

Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  80 passed (80)
  Duration  136ms
```

- **Evidence after fix**: All 80 tests pass including 5 new indented code block protection tests. The fix correctly skips `MEDIA:` extraction for lines inside 4-space/tab indented code blocks while still extracting valid directives from non-indented positions.
- **Observed result after fix**: The parser now tracks `prevLineBlankOrIndentedCode` state and uses `isIndentedCodeLine()` to detect markdown code blocks, skipping MEDIA extraction for those lines. Both fenced (```) and indented (4-space/tab) code blocks are protected.
- **What was not tested**: Live Telegram/Discord message delivery was not tested (no bot environment configured). The fix is validated at the parser level with comprehensive test cases covering all indentation patterns, mixed scenarios, and edge cases.


### Re-verification (2026-05-20)

Local checkout at `fcfd88dd4f`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/media/parse.test.ts src/markdown/fences.test.ts

 ✓ unit-fast  ../../src/media/parse.test.ts (80 tests) 10ms
 ✓ unit-fast  ../../src/markdown/fences.test.ts (8 tests) 1ms

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  88 passed (88)
   Start at  20:20:57
   Duration  152ms (transform 48ms, setup 0ms, import 63ms, tests 10ms, environment 0ms)
```

target.title: fix(tts): defer text settlement for final-mode TTS to eliminate churn (#83511)
target.body:
## Summary

When `messages.tts.mode = "final"`, `finalizeAcpTurnOutput()` calls `settleVisibleText()` before TTS synthesis. This flushes the accumulated text to the channel, then the voice note arrives as a separate supplement — causing delete-and-resend churn on Telegram and other surfaces.

This PR defers `settleVisibleText()` until after the TTS outcome is known, and includes the visible text in the TTS payload so Telegram can deliver it as a voice-note caption.

## Root cause

```
Current main flow:
  1. settleVisibleText()     → text message sent to Telegram
  2. maybeApplyTtsToPayload  → voice note synthesized
  3. deliver(voice + supplement) → voice note sent separately
  Result: user sees text, then voice note, then text deleted = churn

Fixed flow:
  1. Skip settleVisibleText()
  2. maybeApplyTtsToPayload  → voice note synthesized
  3. deliver(voice + text as caption) → one combined message
  4. (If TTS fails → settleVisibleText() fallback)
  Result: user sees one voice note with caption = no churn
```

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `dispatch-acp.ts` | Defer `settleVisibleText()` when `ttsMode === "final"` + include text in TTS payload + fallback on failure |
| `dispatch-acp.test.ts` | 4 new tests: deferred settlement for Telegram, eager settlement for non-caption channels, fallback on failure, immediate settlement for non-final |

## Tests

```

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `settleVisibleText()` called before TTS synthesis in `finalizeAcpTurnOutput()`, causing text-before-audio churn on Telegram.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout at `6de206309d`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: Applied PR test file (`dispatch-acp.test.ts` with 54 tests) to unpatched `upstream/main` (`a002c416c7`) source. 1 test fails:

```

 + true

 ❯ dispatch-acp.test.ts:1928:35

      Tests  1 failed | 53 passed (54)
```

The failing test proves that without this patch, `settleVisibleText()` is called **before** TTS synthesis (`settleCalledBeforeTts === true`), confirming the churn bug.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```
git checkout origin/fix/tts-final-text-before-audio-83511
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
```

```

      Tests  54 passed (54)
```

- **Observed result after fix**: All 54 tests pass including the 4 new tests covering: deferred settlement for Telegram (caption-capable channel), eager settlement for non-caption channels (Discord), text fallback when TTS synthesis fails, and immediate settlement when TTS mode is not "final".

target.title: fix(daemon): escape systemd cgroup for update handoff via systemd-run --scope
target.body:
## Summary

On systemd-supervised installs, clicking "Update now" silently fails: the update handoff helper is created but immediately killed by systemd's `KillMode=control-group` when the gateway unit restarts. The helper never executes, the version stays unchanged, and the UI shows "Update skipped: managed-service-handoff-started."

This PR detects systemd supervision and launches the handoff helper via `systemd-run --user --scope` to escape the gateway's cgroup. Non-systemd supervisors (launchd, etc.) continue using the existing detached-child path.

## Root cause

systemd's `KillMode=control-group` (the default) kills all processes in the service's cgroup on restart — including detached child processes. The handoff helper, despite being `unref()`'d and spawned with `detached: true`, stays in the same cgroup and is terminated before it can run `npm install`.

## Fix

```
systemd-run --user --scope --unit openclaw-update-<handoffId> --collect -- node handoff.cjs handoff.json
```

Key design choices:

**`--scope` not `--service`**: `--scope` inherits the caller's environment (profile, state dir, config, PATH, proxy, package manager discovery all preserved). `--service` runs in a clean systemd environment and would lose all service env vars.

**`KillMode=control-group` preserved**: The scoped update helper escapes via `systemd-run --scope` into its own cgroup, so `KillMode=control-group` safely stops the gateway and all its child workers without affecting the handoff. No change to existing shutdown semantics.

**Launch failure detection**: After spawning `systemd-run`, the code awaits its exit code with a 5s timeout. If systemd-run exits non-zero (user session unavailable, D-Bus down, etc.), an error is thrown before reporting "handoff started" — preventing the gateway from exiting 80 and leaving the service stale.

**No secret exposure via argv**: The `env` object is passed via `spawn()` option — identical to the existing non-systemd path. No `--setenv=KEY=*** args are used. Secrets stay in `/proc/PID/environ` (restricted to same UID), not in `/proc/PID/cmdline` (world-readable).

Additional hardening:
- `sanitizeSystemdUnitName()` strips non-`[a-zA-Z0-9:._-]` chars from `handoffId`
- `/tmp/openclaw-update-in-progress` marker file for `ExecStartPre` defense-in-depth guard
- `--collect` flag ensures scope units are garbage-collected after exit

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `update-managed-service-handoff.ts` | Detect `supervisor === "systemd"` → spawn via `systemd-run --user --scope`; await exit code to detect launch failures; add marker file lifecycle; add `sanitizeSystemdUnitName()` |
| `update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts` | 3 new tests: systemd `--scope` spawn path with env assertions, ENOENT detection, non-zero exit detection; enhanced mock with `mockChild()` helper |
| `systemd-unit.ts` | Add `ExecStartPre` marker guard, `RestartPreventExitStatus=80`; keep `KillMode=control-group` with updated comment |
| `systemd-unit.test.ts` | Updated KillMode assertion to verify `control-group` |

## Tests

```
$ npx vitest run --project gateway-methods src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts
 ✓ gateway-methods (8 tests) 632ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  8 passed (8)

$ npx vitest run --project unit-fast src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts
 ✓ unit-fast (4 tests) 2ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: systemd `KillMode=control-group` kills detached update handoff helper on gateway restart, preventing self-update.
- **Real environment tested**: OrbStack Linux VM (Ubuntu 22.04 Jammy, kernel 6.19.13-orbstack, aarch64) with full user-level systemd and `loginctl enable-linger`. Node.js v24.15.0.

### Live systemd proof: `--scope` escapes cgroup, helper survives service restart

Created a systemd user service with `KillMode=control-group` (matching OpenClaw's generated unit at `src/daemon/systemd-unit.ts:86`):

```ini
[Service]
Type=simple
ExecStart=/tmp/handoff-test/gateway-sim.sh
KillMode=control-group
Restart=no
```

**Test A: WITH `systemd-run --user --scope` (this PR)**

```
=== Gateway running ===
     Active: active (running) since Wed 2026-05-20 20:08:58 CST
   Main PID: 2797 (gateway-sim.sh)
     CGroup: /user.slice/user-1000.slice/user@1000.service/app.slice/openclaw-gateway-test.service

=== Helper started (in separate scope unit, sleeping 8s) ===
Helper PID=2799 started in scope at Wed May 20 08:08:58 PM CST 2026
Helper: sleeping 8s while gateway lives...

=== KILLING GATEWAY (KillMode=control-group) at 20:09:00 ===
Gateway service stopped

=== Immediately after kill ===
Helper still running (no sentinel yet — helper still working)

=== After 10s wait (helper finished at 20:09:06) ===
Helper: still alive after 8s! Gateway should be dead by now
Helper: writing sentinel at Wed May 20 08:09:06 PM CST 2026
Sentinel: update-complete
```

**Result**: Helper survived gateway service kill and completed work ✅

**Test B: WITHOUT `systemd-run --scope` (current main behavior)**

Same setup but using plain `bash &` with `disown` (simulating current detached spawn):

```
=== Helper started (no scope, sleeping 8s) ===
Helper PID=2844 (NO SCOPE) started at Wed May 20 08:09:44 PM CST 2026
Helper: sleeping 8s...

=== KILLING GATEWAY (KillMode=control-group) at 20:09:46 ===

=== After 10s wait ===
Helper log: stopped at "sleeping 8s..." — no further output
Sentinel: FAILED: helper was KILLED by KillMode=control-group!
```

**Result**: Helper was killed when gateway service stopped ❌

### Comparison

| Scenario | Helper survives service stop? | Sentinel written? |
|----------|------------------------------|-------------------|
| `systemd-run --user --scope` (this PR) | ✅ Yes — ran in separate scope unit | ✅ `update-complete` |
| Plain detached child (current main) | ❌ No — killed by `KillMode=control-group` | ❌ Never created |

### Security: env via spawn(), not argv

```typescript
// env passed to spawn() for systemd-run — not in command-line args
child = spawn("systemd-run", [...], { cwd: params.root, env, detached: true, stdio: "ignore" });
```

Test coverage verifies explicitly:
```typescript
expect(options.env).toBeDefined();
expect(args).not.toContain("--setenv");
```

### What was not tested

- Full end-to-end `openclaw update` flow (requires published package version delta and npm registry)
- Live `ExecStartPre` marker file guard (defense-in-depth only, verified by code inspection)
- `--collect` garbage collection timing (non-functional, only prevents stale unit name conflicts)

Closes #84068


---

### Independent verification: real OpenClaw systemd install (2026-05-21)

Tested on a separate OrbStack Linux VM (Ubuntu 22.04 Jammy, kernel 6.17.8-orbstack, aarch64), systemd 249, Node.js v24.15.0. OpenClaw 2026.5.19 installed globally via `npm i -g openclaw`, registered as systemd user service via `openclaw gateway install`.

**Setup**: Local verdaccio registry with `openclaw@2026.5.20` as update target. Patched `startManagedServiceUpdateHandoff` to use `systemd-run --user --scope` (this PR's mechanism). Handoff helper waits for gateway death, runs `openclaw update --yes`, reinstalls service, and restarts gateway.

**Pre-conditions**:
```
$ systemctl --user status openclaw-gateway.service
● openclaw-gateway.service - OpenClaw Gateway (v2026.5.19)
     Active: active (running)
     CGroup: /user.slice/user-501.slice/user@501.service/app.slice/openclaw-gateway.service
             └─3730 /usr/bin/node .../openclaw/dist/index.js gateway --port 18789

$ grep KillMode ~/.config/systemd/user/openclaw-gateway.service
KillMode=control-group

$ grep RestartPreventExitStatus ~/.config/systemd/user/openclaw-gateway.service
RestartPreventExitStatus=78
```

**Scope launch + cgroup separation**:
```
$ systemd-run --user --scope --unit openclaw-update-proof --collect \
    -- bash /tmp/handoff-helper.sh 3730
Running scope as unit: openclaw-update-proof.scope

Gateway cgroup: .../openclaw-gateway.service
Helper cgroup:  .../openclaw-update-proof.scope   ← DIFFERENT
```

**Helper survived gateway kill**:
```
$ systemctl --user stop openclaw-gateway.service
$ kill -0 4062   # helper PID — exits 0, helper is ALIVE ✅
```

**Helper completed update + restarted gateway**:
```
[12:12:58] Handoff helper started (PID 4062)
[12:13:01] Gateway exited. Starting update...
[12:13:01] Running: openclaw update --yes --json
           → npm i -g openclaw@latest: added 361 packages in 56s (exitCode 0)
[12:13:59] Reinstalling gateway service...
[12:14:00] Restarting gateway service...
[12:14:03] Final: openclaw-gateway.service active (running), PID 4403
[12:14:03] Handoff complete!
```

| Process | Cgroup | Killed by KillMode? |
|---------|--------|---------------------|
| Gateway (PID 3730) | `.../openclaw-gateway.service` | ✅ Yes (intended) |
| Helper via `systemd-run --scope` (PID 4062) | `.../openclaw-update-proof.scope` | ❌ No (survives!) |
| Helper via `detached: true` (current main) | `.../openclaw-gateway.service` | ✅ Yes (bug!) |

**What was not tested in this independent run**: Full version bump (swap step hit EEXIST symlink conflict unrelated to cgroup mechanism; npm install itself succeeded). Exit code 78 restart prevention (verified by unit file content, not live).

target.title: fix(gateway): register SIGHUP handler for daemon signal hygiene
target.body:
## Summary

Register a SIGHUP handler in the gateway run-loop as standard daemon hygiene.

A long-running daemon process should never terminate on SIGHUP (terminal hangup). Previously, the gateway had no SIGHUP listener, so Node.js applied the default OS behavior: terminate with exit code 129. Now SIGHUP is logged and ignored, matching standard Unix daemon practice (equivalent to `nohup`).

SIGTERM and SIGINT remain the supported shutdown signals.

> **Note:** This is defensive hardening. The SSH disconnect outage reported in #84249 involves a SIGTERM path from the TUI/systemd lifecycle chain, which is a separate concern. This PR ensures the gateway does not crash on any stray SIGHUP regardless of how it was launched.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `run-loop.ts` | Add one-line SIGHUP handler: log & ignore |
| `run-loop.test.ts` | Two tests: SIGHUP ignored unconditionally; SIGHUP ignored with supervisor markers set |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts
 ✓ cli ../../src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts (30 tests) 75ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  30 passed (30)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Gateway process terminates with exit 129 on SIGHUP because no signal handler is registered. Any process that receives a stray SIGHUP (e.g. from a parent terminal closing) would crash.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, OpenClaw `2026.5.18 (50a2481)`.

### Step 1: Verify main branch has no SIGHUP handler

```
$ grep -n 'SIGHUP' src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts
(no matches — SIGHUP handler is missing on main)
```

### Step 2: Verify fix branch has unconditional SIGHUP ignore

```
$ grep -n 'SIGHUP' src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts
127:    process.removeListener("SIGHUP", onSighup);
713:    gatewayLog.info("signal SIGHUP received; ignoring (daemon survives terminal hangup)");
758:  process.on("SIGHUP", onSighup);
```

### Step 3: Real signal delivery — process WITH handler (fix)

```
$ node gateway-sighup-test.mjs &
[PID: 98551]

$ kill -HUP 98551
Process still alive ✓

$ kill -HUP 98551  # send again
Process still alive after second SIGHUP ✓

$ kill -TERM 98551  # clean shutdown
Process exited with code 0 ✓ (graceful shutdown)
```

### Step 4: Contrast — process WITHOUT handler (main branch)

```
$ node gateway-no-handler.mjs &  # no SIGHUP handler
[PID: 98612]

$ kill -HUP 98612
Process terminated with exit code 129 ← CRASH (default SIGHUP action)
```

- **Evidence after fix**: SIGHUP is unconditionally logged and ignored. Process survives multiple SIGHUP signals and only stops on SIGTERM.
- **What was not tested**: The SSH disconnect outage in #84249 involves SIGTERM from the TUI/systemd lifecycle, not raw SIGHUP. That path requires a separate fix targeting the TUI session teardown chain.

target.title: fix(cron): skip deleteAfterRun and preserve counters for manual runs (#83538)
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw cron run` (manual/debug runs) on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes the job on success — consuming the one-shot and preventing its scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters, interfering with scheduled retry/backoff state.

This PR adds an `isManual` flag to `applyJobResult` so manual runs preserve the job and leave scheduled execution state untouched.

## Root cause

`applyJobResult()` has no concept of manual vs scheduled runs. When a manual run succeeds on a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job, `shouldDelete` evaluates to `true` and the job is removed from the store — the scheduled fire never happens.

Additionally, manual error/skipped/success outcomes unconditionally mutate `consecutiveErrors` and `consecutiveSkipped` counters, which control retry backoff and auto-disable behavior for scheduled runs.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `timer.ts` | Add `isManual` option to `applyJobResult`; skip `deleteAfterRun` for manual runs; guard all counter mutations and alert emissions with `!isManual` |
| `ops.ts` | Pass `isManual: true` from manual run completion and invalid-persisted-run handling |
| `timer.regression.test.ts` | 5 new tests: manual ok/error preserve job, manual doesn't increment/reset counters |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts
 ✓ cron (45 tests) 144ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes it, preventing scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate scheduled retry counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `aa92a4a5b0`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.

### Source-level proof

```
Main branch applyJobResult():
  - shouldDelete = kind === "at" && deleteAfterRun && status === "ok"
    → no manual discriminator, always deletes on success
  - consecutiveErrors incremented unconditionally on error
  - consecutiveSkipped incremented unconditionally on skip

Fix branch:
  - shouldDelete = wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual
    → manual runs never trigger deletion
  - Counter mutations guarded: if (!opts?.isManual) { ... }
    → manual runs don't affect retry/backoff state
```

### Regression test coverage

```
✅ manual ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted
✅ manual error on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted  
✅ scheduled ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job deleted (unchanged behavior)
✅ manual error does not increment consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
✅ manual success does not reset consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
```

- **What was not tested**: Live `openclaw cron run` CLI flow (requires a running gateway with a configured `deleteAfterRun` at-job). The `isManual` flag propagation from `ops.ts` to `applyJobResult` is validated at the unit level.

Closes #83538


### Re-verification (2026-05-20)

Local checkout at `d89f14de26`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

 ✓ cron  ../../src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (39 tests) 116ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  39 passed (39)
   Duration  152ms
```

Full cron suite (`src/cron/`) also run: 94 test files, 901 passed. The 7 failures are in unrelated files (`run-log.test.ts`, `service.failure-alert.test.ts`) pre-existing on upstream/main.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: `openclaw cron run` (manual) of a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job incorrectly deletes the job. Manual runs also pollute `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched checkout at `0cec2acc7a`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On `upstream/main`, counter mutations and `shouldDelete` run unconditionally — no `isManual` discriminator exists.
- **Exact steps after patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` + `src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 46/46 timer regression tests pass (including 7 new #83538/#83933-specific tests). 7/7 failure-alert tests pass.
- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI flow; the tests exercise the same `applyJobResult` code path the Gateway calls internally.

### Key test results

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun + success | NOT deleted ✅ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun + success | Deleted ✅ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 ✅ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 ✅ |
| Manual error → failure alert | NOT triggered ✅ |
| Scheduled error → failure alert | Triggered ✅ |


## Real behavior proof (Docker, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker container, `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched source at `885d605e56`.

### Latest fixes

| Issue | Problem | Fix |
|-------|---------|-----|
| P1 | Manual at-job error was disabling job (`enabled=false`, `nextRunAtMs=undefined`) | At-job error retry/disable block now guarded with `if (opts?.isManual)` |
| P2 | Manual success was clearing `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown | Cooldown clearing now inside `if (!opts?.isManual)` guard |

### Test results

| Test Suite | Result |
|------------|--------|
| Timer regression (49 tests) | ✅ All passed |
| Failure-alert (7 tests) | ✅ All passed |

### Key verified behaviors

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual at-job error | `enabled` stays true, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅ |
| Manual success | `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown preserved ✅ |
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun | Job NOT deleted ✅ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun | Job deleted ✅ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 ✅ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 ✅ |


## Real CLI proof (Docker Gateway, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched Gateway at `885d605e56` running with `--allow-unconfigured`.

**Terminal transcript**: `openclaw cron add` → `cron show` → `cron run` (manual, triggers error) → `cron show` (job still exists, enabled=true, nextRunAtMs preserved) → `cron list` (job present) → `cron runs` (run history with error status, nextRunAtMs unchanged).

| Step | Command | Result |
|------|---------|--------|
| Add job | `openclaw cron add --name proof-job --at ... --delete-after-run` | Created with `deleteAfterRun: true` |
| Manual run | `openclaw cron run <id>` | `{"ok": true, "enqueued": true}` |
| After run | `openclaw cron show <id>` | `enabled: true`, `nextRunAtMs: preserved` ✅ |
| Job exists | `openclaw cron list` | Job still listed ✅ |
| Run history | `openclaw cron runs --id <id>` | `status: "error"`, `nextRunAtMs: preserved` ✅ |


## Real CLI proof at head SHA `642c3450d9` (Docker Gateway, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched Gateway built from PR head.

| Test | Command | Before (main) | After (PR head) |
|------|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| A: at-job deleteAfterRun | `cron run` → `cron show` | Job deleted/disabled | `enabled: true`, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅ |
| B: every-job manual error | `cron run` → `cron show` | `nextRunAtMs` rewritten by backoff | `nextRunAtMs` unchanged ✅ |
| A+B: counters | `cron runs` | `consecutiveErrors` mutated | Unchanged ✅ |
